cards.front: What is the structure (in fields, in order) of an Ethernet II frame?
cards.back: Preamble (7 bytes) | SFD (1) | Destination MAC (6) | Source MAC (6) | Type/EtherType (2) | Data + Pad (46-1500) | FCS (4). The 802.3 variant replaces Type with a Length field.

cards.front: How long is a MAC address and how is it formatted?
cards.back: 48 bits = 6 bytes, written as 12 hex digits. Cisco format groups them in 4-character chunks separated by dots (e.g., 0200.1111.2222); IEEE uses 6 hyphen-separated pairs.

cards.front: What's the difference between unicast, broadcast, and multicast Ethernet addresses?
cards.back: Unicast: one specific NIC; the I/G bit (least-significant bit of the 1st byte) is 0. Broadcast: ffff.ffff.ffff (all bits 1) — sent to every host in the broadcast domain. Multicast: I/G bit = 1, range 0100.5e00.0000–0100.5e7f.ffff for IPv4 multicast mappings.

cards.front: What is the FCS field in an Ethernet frame and what does it do?
cards.back: Frame Check Sequence — 4-byte CRC trailer used for error detection. The receiver recomputes the CRC over the frame; if it doesn't match the FCS, the frame is silently discarded. Ethernet does not retransmit; upper layers (TCP) handle that.

cards.front: Distinguish a collision domain from a broadcast domain.
cards.back: Collision domain = a set of devices whose frames can collide on the wire. Hubs/repeaters extend collision domains; switches break them (each switch port is its own collision domain). Broadcast domain = a set of devices that receive each other's broadcasts; switches extend broadcast domains, routers (or VLAN boundaries) break them.

cards.front: What's the difference between straight-through and crossover UTP cabling, and when do you use each?
cards.back: Straight-through: same pinout each end (T568A↔A or B↔B); used between unlike devices (PC↔switch, switch↔router). Crossover: one end T568A, other end T568B; used between like devices (switch↔switch, router↔router, PC↔router, PC↔PC). Modern Auto-MDIX makes this irrelevant on most ports — but the exam still tests it.

cards.front: What are the minimum and maximum payload sizes for an Ethernet frame, and what does this mean for short messages?
cards.back: Minimum 46 bytes, maximum 1500 bytes (the standard MTU). Frames smaller than 46 bytes get padded. Total frame size including header/FCS: 64–1518 bytes (1522 with 802.1Q tag).

cards.front: What are the 4 standard UTP categories used on the CCNA, and their max bandwidth?
cards.back: Cat 5: 100 Mbps (legacy). Cat 5e: 1 Gbps. Cat 6: 1 Gbps over 100 m, up to 10 Gbps over 55 m. Cat 6a: 10 Gbps over 100 m. Cat 8: 25/40 Gbps over 30 m (data center).

cards.front: What is the OUI portion of a MAC address?
cards.back: Organizationally Unique Identifier — the first 24 bits (3 bytes) of a MAC, assigned to the NIC vendor by IEEE. The remaining 24 bits are vendor-assigned to make each MAC globally unique.

cards.front: What Cisco product line names map to: switch, router, firewall?
cards.back: Switch: Catalyst. Router: ISR (Integrated Services Router). Firewall: ASA (legacy) and Firepower (next-generation, NGFW). NGFW = traditional firewall + advanced filtering (app-level inspection, IPS).

cards.front: Match each IEEE 802.3 amendment to its Ethernet speed/standard: 802.3i, 802.3u, 802.3ab, 802.3an, 802.3z, 802.3ae.
cards.back: 802.3i = 10BASE-T (10 Mbps over UTP). 802.3u = 100BASE-T (Fast Ethernet). 802.3ab = 1000BASE-T (Gigabit over UTP). 802.3an = 10GBASE-T. 802.3z = 1000BASE-X (Gigabit fiber, e.g. 1000BASE-LX). 802.3ae = 10G fiber (10GBASE-SR/LR/ER).

cards.front: How many UTP wire pairs are used by 10/100BASE-T vs 1000/10GBASE-T?
cards.back: 10BASE-T and 100BASE-T use 2 pairs (4 wires) — pins 1-2 and 3-6. 1000BASE-T and 10GBASE-T use all 4 pairs (8 wires) — pins 1-2, 3-6, 4-5, 7-8.

cards.front: On a FastEthernet RJ45 connection, which devices transmit on pins 1-2 vs 3-6?
cards.back: Endpoints/'MDI' devices (PC, router, firewall) transmit on 1-2, receive on 3-6. Switches/'MDI-X' devices transmit on 3-6, receive on 1-2. That's why a straight-through cable works between unlike (PC-switch) and a crossover is needed between like (switch-switch, router-router, PC-router).

A switch/NIC feature that automatically detects and adjusts which RJ45 pin pairs it uses to transmit vs receive, so a straight-through cable works regardless of device pairing. Most modern Gigabit ports support it; the exam still tests the manual cable rules.

cards.front: How are fiber-optic cables connected to a router or switch?
cards.back: Via SFP (Small Form-Factor Pluggable) transceivers — modular optics that plug into an SFP slot on the device. Variants include SFP+ (10G), QSFP (40G), QSFP28 (100G).

cards.front: Single-mode vs multimode fiber — core size, light path, distance, cost?
cards.back: Single-mode: narrow core (~9 µm), light travels at one angle, longer distances (km+), more expensive — uses laser. Multimode: wider core (50/62.5 µm), light enters at multiple angles (modes), shorter distance, cheaper — uses LED or VCSEL.
10GBASE-SR: ~400 m (multimode, Short Range). 10GBASE-LR: 10 km (single-mode, Long Range). 10GBASE-ER: 30-40 km (single-mode, Extended Range). 1000BASE-LX: 550 m multimode / 5 km single-mode.

cards.front: Why is fiber more secure than UTP copper?
cards.back: UTP cables emit a faint EM signal outside the cable that can be detected/copied (eavesdropping) and are vulnerable to EMI. Fiber transmits light through glass — no EM emission, no EMI susceptibility, much harder to tap.

cards.front: What does the speed label 'Ethernet / FastEthernet / GigabitEthernet / 10 GigE' mean in Mbps/Gbps?
cards.back: Ethernet = 10 Mbps. FastEthernet = 100 Mbps. GigabitEthernet = 1 Gbps (1000 Mbps). 10 GigabitEthernet = 10 Gbps. (Cisco interface names: Ethernet0, FastEthernet0/1, GigabitEthernet0/0, TenGigabitEthernet0/1.)
